sections.text: Welcome back to AP Biology. This is Module Two — Cell Structure and Function. It maps to Unit Two of the College Board curriculum, and it shows up on roughly ten to thirteen percent of the AP exam. We just built the molecules of life. Now we're going to put them inside the smallest unit that actually counts as alive — the cell.

Your body is made of about thirty-seven trillion cells. And every single one of them is tiny — most are only ten to a hundred micrometers across. There's a reason for that, and it's not because evolution couldn't make them bigger. It's a geometry problem. By the end of this module you'll know exactly why a cell can't just keep growing — and what evolution invented to get around it.

Here's the game plan. First — the two big categories of cells, prokaryotes and eukaryotes. Second — a tour of the organelles inside eukaryotic cells, and how they form little factories with different jobs. Third — the two big ideas that tie it all together: surface area to volume ratio, and endosymbiotic theory. Three pieces, and you'll have the architecture of every living thing.

Two kinds of cells. Prokaryotes — bacteria and archaea — are small, usually a half micrometer to five micrometers across. No nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles, one circular chromosome floating in a region called the nucleoid, and smaller seventy-S ribosomes. Eukaryotes — protists, plants, fungi, and animals — are ten to a hundred times bigger, with a true membrane-bound nucleus, lots of internal organelles, multiple linear chromosomes, and larger eighty-S ribosomes in their cytoplasm. Same chemistry, very different architecture.

Eukaryotic cells solve a problem prokaryotes don't have. They're big — too big for one big bag of chemistry to work well. So they compartmentalize. Each membrane-bound organelle is like a separate room with its own conditions, its own enzymes, its own job. That's compartmentalization, and it's the single most important idea in this whole module.

Start with the control room. The nucleus holds the DNA, surrounded by a double membrane called the nuclear envelope, with nuclear pores that let messenger RNA out and proteins in. Inside the nucleus you'll see a dense spot — that's the nucleolus, where ribosomal RNA is made and ribosome subunits are assembled. Once those subunits leave the nucleus, ribosomes do one job — build proteins. Free ribosomes in the cytosol make proteins that stay in the cell. Bound ribosomes — stuck to the rough ER — make proteins destined for membranes, lysosomes, or export.

Now the assembly line. The endomembrane system is one connected network. Rough ER, studded with ribosomes, synthesizes and folds membrane and secreted proteins. Smooth ER — no ribosomes — makes lipids and steroids, stores calcium in muscle cells, and detoxifies drugs in liver cells. Both pinch off vesicles that travel to the Golgi apparatus. The Golgi modifies, sorts, and packages those proteins — cis face receives, trans face ships out. From there, vesicles deliver to lysosomes, to the plasma membrane, or out of the cell. Lysosomes are the recycling center — acidic, full of hydrolytic enzymes, and present in animal cells.

Now the power plants. Mitochondria run the oxygen-requiring parts of cellular respiration. Glycolysis — the first step — actually happens in the cytosol, breaking glucose into pyruvate. The pyruvate and oxygen then enter the mitochondria, which generate the bulk of the cell's ATP. They show up in nearly all eukaryotic cells. They have a double membrane — a smooth outer one, and a folded inner one whose folds are called cristae, where the ATP machinery lives. Chloroplasts run photosynthesis — they capture light energy and make glucose. They're found in plants and algae only — never in animal cells. They also have a double membrane, plus internal thylakoid membranes stacked into structures called grana. Hold onto that double-membrane detail — we'll come back to it in a few minutes.

The cytoskeleton is the cell's internal scaffolding — three filament types, each with its own job. Microfilaments are made of actin — they give the cell its shape, drive muscle contraction, and pinch the cell in two during cytokinesis. Intermediate filaments are the strong cables — keratin in your skin, for example — providing mechanical strength. Microtubules are hollow tubes of tubulin — they're the highways for motor proteins like kinesin and dynein, they form the mitotic spindle that pulls chromosomes apart, and they're the structural core of cilia and flagella.

Predict this. A liver cell has one of its main jobs as detoxifying drugs and alcohol. Which three organelles would you expect that liver cell to have lots of? Think about which organelle does detox, which one supplies the energy for that work, and which one handles the toxic byproduct hydrogen peroxide. Pause now. Write your answer down. Press play when you're ready.

Answers. One — smooth ER, because it's where drug-detoxification enzymes live. Two — mitochondria, because detox reactions burn ATP, and the liver cell needs lots of energy to do that work. Three — peroxisomes, because they break down fatty acids and they neutralize hydrogen peroxide using the enzyme catalase. Structure follows function — every time a cell does a job a lot, it builds more of the organelle that does it.

Here's the geometry problem from the beginning of the lesson. As a cell grows, its volume increases as the radius cubed. But the surface area — the membrane that lets nutrients in and waste out — only grows as the radius squared. So volume outruns surface area. The bigger the cell, the smaller the surface-area-to-volume ratio becomes — and at some point, diffusion across the membrane can no longer keep up with the cell's metabolic demand. That's why cells stay small. Larger cells get around it three ways: folding the membrane to add surface area, flattening their shape, or compartmentalizing with organelles — which is exactly what eukaryotic cells did.

Now the origin story. Endosymbiotic theory says mitochondria and chloroplasts used to be free-living prokaryotes, engulfed by a larger host cell about one-and-a-half to two billion years ago, and they never left. Four pieces of evidence support this. One — they have a double membrane, which is what you'd expect if a host vesicle engulfed a bacterium. Two — they have their own circular DNA, like bacteria do. Three — they replicate by binary fission, independent of the host cell cycle. And four — they have seventy-S ribosomes, the prokaryote-sized ribosome. Four clues, one origin story.

Two AP traps that catch students every year. On the left — wrong assumptions. People say lysosomes are in every cell — they're not, they're mainly found in animal cells. People say chloroplasts are in all eukaryotes — they're not, only plants and algae. On the right — the right idea. Plant cells don't have lysosomes, but they do have a large central vacuole that does similar digestive and storage work. Read the question carefully — animal versus plant matters.

Recap. Prokaryotes are small and have no membrane-bound organelles; eukaryotes are big and compartmentalized. Each organelle is a room with a job — nucleus stores DNA, ribosomes build proteins, rough ER and Golgi process and ship them, smooth ER makes lipids and detoxifies, mitochondria make ATP, chloroplasts make glucose in plants, and the cytoskeleton holds it all together. Surface-area-to-volume ratio is why cells stay small. And mitochondria and chloroplasts trace back to free-living prokaryotes — that's endosymbiotic theory, with four pieces of evidence.

Your path forward. Read OpenStax Biology Chapter Four for the deeper version of every organelle we covered. Drill the Khan Academy AP Biology Unit Two problem sets to lock in the structure-to-function connections. Do the assignment in your dashboard. And book an advisor check-in if the endomembrane flow or the endosymbiosis evidence still feels fuzzy — those are the two pieces students miss most. Next time, we move into Module Three — Membrane Transport, where we'll open up that plasma membrane and see how things cross it.
